content:
# Luke Chapter 19 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 111-113 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_019_page_001.jpg, LUK_019_page_002.jpg, LUK_019_page_003.jpg

---

**1** And having entered, he was passing through Jericho.

**2** And behold, a man called by the name Zacchaeus, and he was a chief tax collector, and he was wealthy.

**3** And he was seeking to see Jesus, who he is, and was not able on account of the crowd, because he was small in stature.

**4** And having run ahead to the front, he climbed up into a sycamore tree, that he might see him, for he was about to pass through that way.

**5** And when he came to the place, Jesus, having looked up, said to him, "Zacchaeus, make haste and come down, for today I must stay at your house."

**6** And he made haste and came down, and received him rejoicing.

**7** And having seen it, they all were grumbling, saying that he had entered to lodge with a sinful man.

**8** And Zacchaeus, having stood, said to the Lord, "Behold, the half of my possessions, Lord, I give to the poor; and if I have defrauded anyone of anything, I restore it fourfold."

**9** And Jesus said to him, "Today salvation has come to this house, inasmuch as he also is a son of Abraham.

**10** For the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost."

**11** And as they were hearing these things, having added thereto, he spoke a parable, because he was near to Jerusalem and they supposed that the kingdom of God was about to appear immediately.

**12** He said therefore, "A certain man of noble birth went to a distant country to receive for himself a kingdom and to return.

**13** And having called ten of his own servants, he gave them ten minas and said to them, 'Conduct business until I come.'

**14** But his citizens hated him and sent a delegation after him, saying, 'We do not want this man to reign over us.'

**15** And it came to pass, when he returned, having received the kingdom, that he commanded these servants to whom he had given the money to be called to him, that he might know what they had gained by trading.

**16** And the first came forward, saying, 'Lord, your mina has earned ten minas.'

**17** And he said to him, 'Well done, good servant! Because you were faithful in a very small thing, have authority over ten cities.'

**18** And the second came, saying, 'Your mina, Lord, has made five minas.'

**19** And he said also to this one, 'And you, be over five cities.'

**20** And the other came, saying, 'Lord, behold your mina, which I kept laid away in a cloth.

**21** For I feared you, because you are a severe man; you take up what you did not lay down, and you reap what you did not sow.'

**22** He says to him, 'Out of your own mouth I will judge you, wicked servant. You knew that I am a severe man, taking up what I did not lay down and reaping what I did not sow?

**23** And why did you not give my money to the bank, and I, having come, would have collected it with interest?'

**24** And to those standing by he said, 'Take the mina from him and give it to the one who has the ten minas.'

**25** And they said to him, 'Lord, he has ten minas!'

**26** 'I say to you that to everyone who has, more shall be given; but from the one who does not have, even what he has shall be taken away.

**27** Moreover, those enemies of mine who did not want me to reign over them — bring them here and slay them before me.'"

**28** And having said these things, he was going on ahead, going up to Jerusalem.

**29** And it came to pass, as he drew near to Bethphage and Bethany, toward the mount that is called Olivet, he sent two of the disciples,

**30** saying, "Go into the village opposite, in which, as you enter, you will find a colt tied, upon which no one of men has ever sat; having loosed it, bring it.

**31** And if anyone asks you, 'Why are you loosing it?' you shall say thus: 'Because the Lord has need of it.'"

**32** And those who had been sent, having departed, found it just as he had said to them.

**33** And as they were loosing the colt, its owners said to them, "Why are you loosing the colt?"

**34** And they said, "Because the Lord has need of it."

**35** And they brought it to Jesus, and having cast their garments upon the colt, they set Jesus upon it.

**36** And as he was going, they were spreading their garments on the road.

**37** And as he was drawing near, already at the descent of the Mount of Olives, the whole multitude of the disciples began rejoicing to praise God with a loud voice concerning all the mighty works which they had seen,

**38** saying, "Blessed is the King who comes in the name of the Lord! Peace in heaven and glory in the highest!"

**39** And some of the Pharisees from the crowd said to him, "Teacher, rebuke your disciples."

**40** And having answered, he said, "I say to you, if these should be silent, the stones will cry out."

**41** And as he drew near, having seen the city, he wept over it,

**42** saying, "If you had known in this day, even you, the things pertaining to peace — but now they are hidden from your eyes.

**43** For days shall come upon you when your enemies will cast up a rampart against you and will surround you and press you in on every side,

**44** and they will dash you to the ground, and your children within you, and they will not leave in you stone upon stone, because you did not know the time of your visitation."

**45** And having entered into the temple, he began to cast out those who were selling,

**46** saying to them, "It is written, 'And my house shall be a house of prayer,' but you have made it a den of robbers."

**47** And he was teaching daily in the temple. But the chief priests and the scribes and the leaders of the people were seeking to destroy him,

**48** and they were not finding what they might do, for all the people were hanging upon him, listening.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Manuscript Verification

The three Sinaiticus manuscript images (pages 111-113) were examined. Page 1 covers approximately Luke 18:22 through Luke 19:5 across four columns, with column markers at Lk. 18:22, Lk. 18:30, Lk. 18:39, and Lk. 19:5. Page 2 covers approximately Luke 19:13 through Luke 19:39 across four columns, with column markers at Lk. 19:13, Lk. 19:22, Lk. 19:30, and Lk. 19:39. Page 3 covers approximately Luke 19:48 through Luke 20:24 across four columns, with column markers at Lk. 19:48, Lk. 20:9, Lk. 20:17, and Lk. 20:24. The text is in Sinaiticus's characteristic continuous uncial script, four columns per page. Key readings confirmed from the manuscript:

- ΚΑΙ ΕΙΣΕΛΘΩΝ ΔΙΗΡΧΕΤΟ ΤΗΝ ΙΕΡΙΧΩ (19:1, "and having entered, he was passing through Jericho")
- ΑΡΧΙΤΕΛΩΝΗΣ (19:2, "chief tax collector" — hapax legomenon)
- ΤΗ ΗΛΙΚΙΑ ΜΙΚΡΟΣ ΗΝ (19:3, "he was small in stature")
- ΣΥΚΟΜΟΡΑΙΑΝ (19:4, variant spelling of "sycamore tree")
- ΣΗΜΕΡΟΝ ΓΑΡ ΕΝ ΤΩ ΟΙΚΩ ΣΟΥ ΔΕΙ ΜΕ ΜΕΙΝΑΙ (19:5, "for today I must stay at your house")
- ΠΑΝΤΕΣ ΔΙΕΓΟΓΓΥΖΟΝ (19:7, "they all were grumbling")
- ΕΙ ΤΙΝΟΣ ΤΙ ΕΣΥΚΟΦΑΝΤΗΣΑ ΑΠΟΔΙΔΩΜΙ ΤΕΤΡΑΠΛΟΥΝ (19:8, "if I have defrauded anyone of anything, I restore fourfold")
- ΣΗΜΕΡΟΝ ΣΩΤΗΡΙΑ ΤΩ ΟΙΚΩ ΤΟΥΤΩ ΕΓΕΝΕΤΟ (19:9, "today salvation has come to this house")
- ΖΗΤΗΣΑΙ ΚΑΙ ΣΩΣΑΙ ΤΟ ΑΠΟΛΩΛΟΣ (19:10, "to seek and to save the lost")
- ΠΑΡΑΧΡΗΜΑ ΜΕΛΛΕΙ Η ΒΑΣΙΛΕΙΑ ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ ΑΝΑΦΑΙΝΕΣΘΑΙ (19:11, "the kingdom of God was about to appear immediately")
- ΔΕΚΑ ΜΝΑΣ (19:13, "ten minas")
- ΟΥ ΘΕΛΟΜΕΝ ΤΟΥΤΟΝ ΒΑΣΙΛΕΥΣΑΙ ΕΦ ΗΜΑΣ (19:14, "we do not want this man to reign over us")
- ΙΣΘΙ ΕΞΟΥΣΙΑΝ ΕΧΩΝ ΕΠΑΝΩ ΔΕΚΑ ΠΟΛΕΩΝ (19:17, "have authority over ten cities")
- ΣΟΥΔΑΡΙΩ (19:20, "cloth/facecloth" — Latin loanword)
- ΑΥΣΤΗΡΟΣ (19:21, "severe/harsh")
- ΕΚ ΤΟΥ ΣΤΟΜΑΤΟΣ ΣΟΥ ΚΡΙΝΩ ΣΕ ΠΟΝΗΡΕ ΔΟΥΛΕ (19:22, "out of your own mouth I will judge you, wicked servant")
- ΚΑΤΑΣΦΑΞΑΤΕ ΑΥΤΟΥΣ ΕΜΠΡΟΣΘΕΝ ΜΟΥ (19:27, "slay them before me")
- ΒΗΘΦΑΓΗ ΚΑΙ ΒΗΘΑΝΙΑΝ (19:29, "Bethphage and Bethany")
- ΠΩΛΟΝ ΔΕΔΕΜΕΝΟΝ ΕΦ ΟΝ ΟΥΔΕΙΣ ΠΩΠΟΤΕ ΑΝΘΡΩΠΩΝ ΕΚΑΘΙΣΕΝ (19:30, "a colt tied, upon which no one of men has ever sat")
- ΕΥΛΟΓΗΜΕΝΟΣ Ο ΕΡΧΟΜΕΝΟΣ Ο ΒΑΣΙΛΕΥΣ ΕΝ ΟΝΟΜΑΤΙ ΚΥΡΙΟΥ (19:38, "blessed is the King who comes in the name of the Lord")
- ΕΙΡΗΝΗ ΕΝ ΟΥΡΑΝΩ ΚΑΙ ΔΟΞΑ ΕΝ ΥΨΙΣΤΟΙΣ (19:38, "peace in heaven and glory in the highest")
- ΟΙ ΛΙΘΟΙ ΚΡΑΞΟΥΣΙΝ (19:40, "the stones will cry out")
- ΕΚΛΑΥΣΕΝ ΕΠ ΑΥΤΗΝ (19:41, "he wept over it")
- ΕΔΑΦΙΟΥΣΙΝ ΣΕ ΚΑΙ ΤΑ ΤΕΚΝΑ ΣΟΥ ΕΝ ΣΟΙ (19:44, "they will dash you to the ground, and your children within you")
- ΤΟΝ ΚΑΙΡΟΝ ΤΗΣ ΕΠΙΣΚΟΠΗΣ ΣΟΥ (19:44, "the time of your visitation")
- ΣΠΗΛΑΙΟΝ ΛΗΣΤΩΝ (19:46, "den of robbers")
- ΕΞΕΚΡΕΜΑΤΟ ΑΥΤΟΥ ΑΚΟΥΩΝ (19:48, "hanging upon him, listening")

### Translation Principles Applied

1. **19:2 — "wealthy" vs. "rich":** EMET renders "wealthy," LOGOS "rich," PNEUMA "very wealthy." SOPHIA follows EMET — "wealthy" renders ΠΛΟΥΣΙΟΣ precisely; LOGOS's "rich" is acceptable but less formal; PNEUMA adds "very" which is not in the Greek (ΣΦΟΔΡΑ is absent here, unlike 18:23).

2. **19:4 — "to the front" vs. "to the [place] before":** EMET renders "to the [place] before," LOGOS "to the front," PNEUMA "ahead." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "to the front" renders ΕΙΣ ΤΟ ΕΜΠΡΟΣΘΕΝ naturally without brackets; EMET's brackets supply unnecessary words; PNEUMA's "ahead" loses the spatial specificity.

3. **19:5 — "make haste and come down" vs. "having hurried, come-down" vs. "come down immediately":** EMET renders "having hurried, come-down," LOGOS "make haste and come down," PNEUMA "come down immediately." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "make haste" renders ΣΠΕΥΣΑΣ as a distinct action naturally; EMET's participial chain is over-literal; PNEUMA collapses two actions into one.

4. **19:7 — "grumbling" vs. "murmuring":** EMET renders "grumbling," LOGOS "murmuring," PNEUMA "grumble." SOPHIA follows EMET and PNEUMA — "grumbling" renders ΔΙΕΓΟΓΓΥΖΟΝ with appropriate force; "murmuring" (LOGOS) is slightly softer than the intensified compound verb warrants.

5. **19:8 — "defrauded" vs. "extorted-from" vs. "cheated":** EMET renders "extorted-from," LOGOS "defrauded," PNEUMA "cheated." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "defrauded" renders ΕΣΥΚΟΦΑΝΤΗΣΑ precisely in formal English; EMET's "extorted-from" is hyphenated and slightly narrow; PNEUMA's "cheated" is too colloquial.

6. **19:10 — "the lost" vs. "the [one] having been lost":** EMET renders "the [one] having been lost," LOGOS "the lost," PNEUMA "the lost." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "the lost" renders ΤΟ ΑΠΟΛΩΛΟΣ concisely; EMET's bracketed expansion is unnecessary.

7. **19:11 — "supposed" vs. "were thinking" vs. "thought":** EMET renders "were thinking," LOGOS "supposed," PNEUMA "thought." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "supposed" renders ΔΟΚΕΙΝ with the appropriate sense of mistaken expectation; EMET's "were thinking" is imprecise; PNEUMA's "thought" is acceptable but "supposed" better captures the error.

8. **19:14 — "citizens" vs. "subjects":** EMET renders "citizens," LOGOS "citizens," PNEUMA "subjects." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "citizens" renders ΠΟΛΙΤΑΙ precisely; PNEUMA's "subjects" presupposes the kingship that has not yet been conferred.

9. **19:15 — "it came to pass" vs. "it came-to-be":** EMET renders "it came-to-be," LOGOS "it came to pass," PNEUMA omits. SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "it came to pass" renders ΕΓΕΝΕΤΟ in SOPHIA's established idiom (cf. 18:35); EMET's "came-to-be" is over-literal; PNEUMA omits the formula entirely.

10. **19:15 — "money" vs. "silver":** EMET renders "silver," LOGOS "money," PNEUMA "money." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "money" renders ΤΟ ΑΡΓΥΡΙΟΝ in its functional sense; EMET's "silver" is the etymological rendering but the word functions as "money" in context.

11. **19:17 — "Well done" vs. "Well [done]":** EMET renders "Well [done]," LOGOS "Well done," PNEUMA "Well done." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "Well done" renders ΕΥΓΕ without brackets; the exclamation is self-explanatory in English.

12. **19:20 — "kept laid away in a cloth" vs. "had lying-away in a face-cloth" vs. "kept wrapped up in a cloth":** EMET renders "had lying-away in a face-cloth," LOGOS "kept laid away in a cloth," PNEUMA "kept wrapped up in a cloth." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "kept laid away in a cloth" renders ΕΙΧΟΝ ΑΠΟΚΕΙΜΕΝΗΝ ΕΝ ΣΟΥΔΑΡΙΩ precisely; EMET's "face-cloth" overtranslates ΣΟΥΔΑΡΙΟΝ in this context; PNEUMA's "wrapped up" is slightly interpretive.

13. **19:21 — "severe" vs. "harsh":** EMET renders "harsh," LOGOS "severe," PNEUMA "harsh." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "severe" renders ΑΥΣΤΗΡΟΣ with precision; "harsh" (EMET, PNEUMA) is acceptable but "severe" better captures the word's sense of strictness and exacting demand.

14. **19:27 — "slay" vs. "execute":** EMET renders "slay," LOGOS "slay," PNEUMA "execute." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "slay" renders ΚΑΤΑΣΦΑΞΑΤΕ with its violent connotation; PNEUMA's "execute" is clinical and loses the visceral force of the intensified compound verb.

15. **19:29 — "the mount that is called Olivet" vs. "the mountain being called [the Mount] of Olives" vs. "the place called the Mount of Olives":** EMET renders "the mountain being called [the Mount] of Olives," LOGOS "the mount that is called Olivet," PNEUMA "the place called the Mount of Olives." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "the mount that is called Olivet" renders ΤΟ ΟΡΟΣ ΤΟ ΚΑΛΟΥΜΕΝΟΝ ΕΛΑΙΩΝ with formal elegance; EMET adds brackets; PNEUMA substitutes "place" for "mount."

16. **19:30 — "no one of men has ever sat" vs. "no man has ever sat" vs. "no one has ever ridden":** EMET renders "no-one of men has ever sat," LOGOS "no man has ever sat," PNEUMA "no one has ever ridden." SOPHIA renders "no one of men has ever sat" — this preserves ΟΥΔΕΙΣ ... ΑΝΘΡΩΠΩΝ faithfully; LOGOS's "no man" is acceptable but loses the partitive construction; PNEUMA's "ridden" interprets ΕΚΑΘΙΣΕΝ ("sat") freely.

17. **19:35 — "cast their garments" vs. "threw their garments" vs. "threw their cloaks":** EMET renders "thrown their garments," LOGOS "cast their garments," PNEUMA "threw their cloaks." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "cast" renders ΕΠΙΡΡΙΨΑΝΤΕΣ with appropriate formality; PNEUMA's "cloaks" replaces ΙΜΑΤΙΑ ("garments") with a specific garment type.

18. **19:37 — "mighty works" vs. "works-of-power" vs. "miracles":** EMET renders "works-of-power," LOGOS "mighty works," PNEUMA "miracles." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "mighty works" renders ΔΥΝΑΜΕΩΝ naturally; EMET's hyphenated form is over-literal; PNEUMA's "miracles" is interpretive.

19. **19:40 — "if these should be silent" vs. "if these shall be-silent" vs. "if they kept silent":** EMET renders "if these shall be-silent," LOGOS "if these should be silent," PNEUMA "if they kept silent." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "should be silent" renders ΕΑΝ ... ΣΙΩΠΗΣΟΥΣΙΝ with appropriate conditionality; EMET hyphenates unnecessarily; PNEUMA's "kept silent" restructures the conditional.

20. **19:42 — "the things pertaining to peace" vs. "the [things leading] to peace" vs. "what would bring you peace":** EMET renders "the [things leading] to peace," LOGOS "the things pertaining to peace," PNEUMA "what would bring you peace." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "the things pertaining to peace" renders ΤΑ ΠΡΟΣ ΕΙΡΗΝΗΝ precisely; EMET adds "leading" in brackets; PNEUMA paraphrases.

21. **19:42 — "they are hidden" vs. "they were hidden":** EMET renders "they were hidden," LOGOS "they are hidden," PNEUMA "it is hidden." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "they are hidden" renders ΕΚΡΥΒΗ as a present-state result of the aorist action, emphasizing the current condition; EMET's "were hidden" emphasizes the past action; the present-state rendering better suits the prophetic lament addressed to Jerusalem in the present moment.

22. **19:43 — "cast up a rampart" vs. "throw-up a palisade" vs. "build a siege wall":** EMET renders "throw-up a palisade," LOGOS "cast up a rampart," PNEUMA "build a siege wall." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "cast up a rampart" renders ΠΑΡΕΜΒΑΛΟΥΣΙΝ ... ΧΑΡΑΚΑ with formal precision; EMET's "throw-up" is less formal; PNEUMA's "build a siege wall" is interpretive.

23. **19:43 — "For days shall come" vs. "Because days will come":** EMET renders "Because days will come," LOGOS "For days shall come," PNEUMA "For the days are coming." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "For" renders ΟΤΙ in its causal sense with formal register consistent with SOPHIA's practice; "shall" preserves prophetic solemnity.

24. **19:44 — "dash you to the ground" vs. "level-you-to-the-ground" vs. "level you to the ground":** EMET renders "level-you-to-the-ground," LOGOS "dash you to the ground," PNEUMA "level you to the ground." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "dash you to the ground" renders ΕΔΑΦΙΟΥΣΙΝ with vivid force; EMET hyphenates; PNEUMA's "level" is acceptable but less vivid.

25. **19:47 — "leaders of the people" vs. "chief-ones of the people" vs. "leading men of the people":** EMET renders "chief-ones of the people," LOGOS "leaders of the people," PNEUMA "leading men of the people." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "leaders" renders ΠΡΩΤΟΙ naturally; EMET's "chief-ones" is hyphenated and awkward; PNEUMA's "leading men" adds gender not in the Greek.

26. **19:48 — "hanging upon him, listening" vs. "hung on his every word":** EMET renders "hanging-upon [him], listening," LOGOS "hanging upon him, listening," PNEUMA "hung on his every word." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "hanging upon him, listening" preserves both ΕΞΕΚΡΕΜΑΤΟ ΑΥΤΟΥ and ΑΚΟΥΩΝ as distinct elements; PNEUMA conflates the two into an English idiom.

---

*Compiled by SOPHIA in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA, from Codex Sinaiticus (4th century CE).*
